steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what point the author makes specifically about Spanish. The passage traces two loanwords (“tomato” and “maize”) from Indigenous languages into Spanish, then from Spanish into English. Spanish is a link in a chain, not the originator.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It has borrowed words from Indigenous languages and contributed words to them.: The passage says Spanish borrowed from Indigenous languages, but it never says Spanish gave words back to those languages. The second half is invented. ✗
  • Choice B — Its contribution to English vocabulary roughly equals the collective contribution by Indigenous languages.: The passage compares no quantities and draws no such equivalence. This is not supported. ✗
  • Choice C — It adopted Nahuatl and Taíno words in approximately equal numbers.: The passage gives one Nahuatl example and one Taíno example, but never claims the numbers are equal. ✗
  • Choice D — It has served as a medium through which Indigenous languages have influenced English.: Both examples show Indigenous words entering Spanish first, then Spanish passing them to English. Spanish functions as the bridge—the medium. This matches exactly. ✓
D is correct.
